questions.q: A pod in the `production` namespace needs to resolve a Service named `cache-svc` in the `backend` namespace by its short name. Which fully qualified domain name (FQDN) will CoreDNS ultimately resolve?
questions.explanation: Kubernetes Service DNS records follow the format <svc>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. To reach a service in a different namespace, the full FQDN including the target namespace must be used: cache-svc.backend.svc.cluster.local.

questions.q: Which Kubernetes component provides cluster-internal DNS resolution and resolves Service names to their ClusterIP addresses?
questions.explanation: CoreDNS runs as a Deployment in the kube-system namespace and acts as the cluster's internal DNS server. It watches the API server for Service resources and creates DNS A records mapping Service names to their ClusterIP addresses.

questions.q: A pod's /etc/resolv.conf is automatically configured by Kubernetes to point to the CoreDNS service IP as the nameserver.
questions.explanation: True. When a pod starts, kubelet automatically injects /etc/resolv.conf into the pod with the CoreDNS ClusterIP (typically 10.96.0.10) as the nameserver and appropriate search domains (e.g. default.svc.cluster.local).
